skills.prompt: This topic covers subcellular components for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles including the nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum (rough and smooth), Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, chloroplasts (in plants), lysosomes, and vacuoles. Each organelle has a specific structure that supports its function: the nucleus houses DNA, ribosomes synthesize proteins, mitochondria perform cellular respiration, and chloroplasts carry out photosynthesis. Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles but contain ribosomes, a cell membrane, and genetic material in a nucleoid region. Students must compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Connect to the endomembrane system, endosymbiotic theory, and cell compartmentalization. AP exam note: questions frequently ask about organelle function and how structure supports that function. Vocabulary: organelle, eukaryote, prokaryote, endomembrane system, ribosome, cytoplasm, nucleoid.

This topic covers cell structure and function for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that all cells share common features: a plasma membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, and genetic material. Eukaryotic cells are distinguished by internal membrane-bound compartments that allow specialized functions. The endomembrane system (ER, Golgi, vesicles, plasma membrane) works together to produce, modify, and transport proteins and lipids. The cytoskeleton (microfilaments, intermediate filaments, microtubules) provides structural support, facilitates movement, and aids in cell division. Students must explain how subcellular structures contribute to cell function and how disruption of any component affects the whole cell. Connect to cell communication, transport, and division. AP exam note: diagrams of cells with labeled organelles are common. Vocabulary: cytoskeleton, microfilament, microtubule, endomembrane system, vesicle, centriole, flagellum, cilium.

This topic covers cell size for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that cell size is limited by the surface area-to-volume ratio. As a cell grows, its volume increases faster than its surface area, reducing the efficiency of nutrient uptake and waste removal across the membrane. This explains why cells remain small or develop adaptations (folding, elongation, microvilli) to increase surface area. Students must calculate and compare surface area-to-volume ratios for different cell shapes and sizes, and explain why larger organisms have more cells rather than larger cells. Connect to metabolic rate, diffusion efficiency, and why mitochondria and chloroplasts have folded internal membranes. AP exam note: mathematical reasoning about SA:V ratios appears frequently. Vocabulary: surface area-to-volume ratio, diffusion, microvilli, metabolic demand.

This topic covers plasma membranes for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand the fluid mosaic model: the membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins, cholesterol, glycoproteins, and glycolipids. Phospholipids are amphipathic, with hydrophilic heads facing aqueous environments and hydrophobic tails forming the interior. Membrane proteins include integral (transmembrane) and peripheral proteins that function in transport, signaling, enzymatic activity, and cell recognition. Cholesterol modulates membrane fluidity across temperature ranges. Students must explain how membrane composition enables selective permeability. Connect to cell signaling, immune recognition, and membrane transport. AP exam note: questions may ask students to predict how changes in membrane composition affect function. Vocabulary: fluid mosaic model, phospholipid bilayer, amphipathic, integral protein, peripheral protein, glycoprotein, cholesterol, selective permeability.

This topic covers membrane permeability for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that the plasma membrane is selectively permeable: small nonpolar molecules (O2, CO2) and water cross freely, while ions and large polar molecules require transport proteins. Permeability depends on molecule size, polarity, and charge. The hydrophobic core of the bilayer acts as a barrier to charged and polar substances. Students must predict which substances can cross the membrane by simple diffusion and which require facilitated transport. Connect to osmosis, facilitated diffusion, and active transport mechanisms. AP exam note: experimental design questions may involve dialysis tubing or artificial membranes to test permeability. Vocabulary: selective permeability, concentration gradient, simple diffusion, hydrophobic, hydrophilic, polar, nonpolar, electrochemical gradient.

This topic covers membrane transport for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand the distinction between passive transport (no energy required, moves down concentration gradient) and active transport (requires ATP, moves against concentration gradient). Passive transport includes simple diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion. Active transport uses membrane pumps such as the sodium-potassium pump (Na+/K+ ATPase). Bulk transport includes endocytosis (phagocytosis, pinocytosis, receptor-mediated) and exocytosis. Students must compare transport mechanisms and predict the direction of molecular movement given concentration data. Connect to nerve impulse transmission, kidney function, and cellular energetics. AP exam note: questions frequently involve interpreting transport diagrams and predicting outcomes. Vocabulary: passive transport, active transport, endocytosis, exocytosis, concentration gradient, ATP, pump, vesicle.

This topic covers facilitated diffusion for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.E, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that facilitated diffusion is passive transport mediated by channel proteins or carrier proteins. Channel proteins form hydrophilic pores for ions and small polar molecules (e.g., aquaporins for water). Carrier proteins undergo conformational changes to shuttle specific molecules across the membrane. This process requires no ATP and moves substances down their concentration gradient. Transport rate is limited by the number of available proteins (saturation kinetics). Students must explain how protein specificity enables selective transport and distinguish facilitated diffusion from simple diffusion and active transport. Connect to glucose transport, ion channels in neurons, and enzyme-like specificity. AP exam note: graphs showing transport rate vs. concentration may test understanding of saturation. Vocabulary: channel protein, carrier protein, aquaporin, saturation, specificity, conformational change.

This topic covers tonicity and osmoregulation for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.F, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). Students should understand that tonicity describes the relative solute concentration of a solution compared to a cell: hypertonic (more solute outside, cell shrinks), hypotonic (less solute outside, cell swells), and isotonic (equal concentrations, no net water movement). In plant cells, the cell wall prevents lysis in hypotonic solutions, maintaining turgor pressure. Water potential (Psi = Psi_p + Psi_s) governs the direction of water movement: water moves from high to low water potential. Students must calculate water potential and predict water movement. Connect to kidney function, plant transpiration, and contractile vacuoles in protists. AP exam note: water potential calculations and experimental design with potato cores are common. Vocabulary: tonicity, hypertonic, hypotonic, isotonic, plasmolysis, turgor, water potential, solute potential, pressure potential.

This topic covers mechanisms of transport for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.G, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). Students should understand that active transport mechanisms use cellular energy to move substances against their concentration gradients. Primary active transport directly uses ATP (e.g., Na+/K+ ATPase). Secondary active transport (cotransport) uses the electrochemical gradient established by primary active transport to move other substances via symporters or antiporters. Bulk transport moves large molecules or particles: endocytosis brings material in, exocytosis releases material. Receptor-mediated endocytosis uses specific receptors for targeted uptake. Students must compare all transport mechanisms and explain energy requirements. Connect to neurotransmitter release, hormone secretion, and nutrient absorption. AP exam note: questions may require identifying transport type from experimental data. Vocabulary: primary active transport, cotransport, symport, antiport, endocytosis, exocytosis, receptor-mediated endocytosis.

This topic covers cell compartmentalization for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.H, Unit 2, Topic 2.10). Students should understand that membrane-bound organelles create distinct chemical environments within eukaryotic cells, allowing incompatible metabolic processes to occur simultaneously. For example, lysosomes maintain an acidic pH for hydrolytic enzymes separate from the neutral cytoplasm. The ER and Golgi form an endomembrane system for protein processing and trafficking. Mitochondrial and chloroplast membranes create compartments essential for chemiosmosis. Students must explain how compartmentalization increases metabolic efficiency and enables cellular specialization. Connect to the evolution of eukaryotic cells (endosymbiotic theory) and to how disruption of compartmentalization causes disease. AP exam note: questions may ask why certain reactions must be separated. Vocabulary: compartmentalization, endomembrane system, chemiosmosis, lysosome, peroxisome, intermembrane space, matrix.

This topic covers the origins of cell compartmentalization for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.11). Students should understand the endosymbiotic theory, which proposes that mitochondria and chloroplasts originated as free-living prokaryotes engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells. Evidence includes: both organelles have double membranes, their own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes (similar to bacteria), and they replicate by binary fission independently of the cell cycle. Mitochondria likely descended from aerobic proteobacteria, and chloroplasts from photosynthetic cyanobacteria. Students must evaluate evidence for endosymbiosis and explain how this theory accounts for structural features of these organelles. Connect to evolution, phylogenetics, and the diversity of eukaryotic cells. AP exam note: questions may present data and ask students to support or refute endosymbiotic theory. Vocabulary: endosymbiosis, endosymbiotic theory, double membrane, circular DNA, binary fission, cyanobacteria, proteobacteria.
